title: Setup Python Environment and Dependencies
description: Install required packages for deep learning, pruning, and membership inference attacks. Use CUDA-enabled PyTorch for GPU acceleration.

steps.step1: Create virtual environment: python -m venv .venv && source .venv/bin/activate
steps.step2: Install PyTorch 2.0+ with CUDA 11.8: pip install torch torchvision --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu118
steps.step3: Install scientific computing: pip install numpy scipy scikit-learn matplotlib seaborn pandas
steps.step4: Install Opacus for DP-SGD: pip install opacus
steps.step5: Verify GPU: python -c 'import torch; print(torch.cuda.is_available(), torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))'

title: Download and Preprocess CIFAR-10 Dataset
description: Download CIFAR-10 and create proper splits for training, validation, and MIA evaluation. The validation set is crucial for computing privacy saliency metrics. Focus on CIFAR-10 only to save time.

steps.step2: Define transforms: train_transform = RandomCrop(32, padding=4) + RandomHorizontalFlip() + ToTensor + Normalize((0.4914, 0.4822, 0.4465), (0.2470, 0.2435, 0.2616))
steps.step3: Create training set: 45,000 samples (90% of official train split), seed=42
steps.step4: Create validation set: 5,000 samples (10% of official train split), seed=42 - used for privacy saliency computation
steps.step5: Create test set: 10,000 samples (official test split) - used for evaluation only
steps.step6: Create MIA evaluation splits: For each experiment, randomly sample 1,000 members from training set and 1,000 non-members from test set
steps.step7: Verify class balance: ~10% per class in all splits

title: Train Unpruned Baseline Models
description: Train ResNet-18 models to convergence on CIFAR-10. These serve as the foundation for all pruning experiments. Reduced to 150 epochs to save time while maintaining good convergence.

steps.step2: Modify first conv: Change from 7x7 to 3x3 kernel, stride 1 (standard for CIFAR)
steps.step3: Remove maxpool layer after first conv (CIFAR-specific modification)
steps.step4: Training config: SGD optimizer, lr=0.1, momentum=0.9, weight_decay=5e-4, batch_size=128
steps.step5: LR schedule: CosineAnnealingLR for 150 epochs (reduced from 200 to save time)
steps.step6: Train 3 models with seeds [42, 43, 44] for statistical significance
steps.step7: Save final model checkpoint and record: train accuracy, test accuracy, model size (MB), FLOPs using thop library

title: Implement Magnitude-Based Pruning Baseline
description: Standard ℓ1-norm based pruning (Han et al., 2015). Simple baseline showing pruning can incidentally improve privacy.

steps.step1: Implement iterative magnitude pruning: prune p% of channels with smallest ℓ1-norm of weights per layer
steps.step2: Pruning schedule: Start from trained baseline, prune in 5 iterations (target sparsity / 5 per iteration)
steps.step3: After each pruning iteration, fine-tune for 8 epochs: lr=0.01 (4 epochs), lr=0.001 (4 epochs)
steps.step4: Target sparsity levels: [0.3, 0.5, 0.7] to cover moderate to high compression
steps.step5: For each sparsity level, train with 3 random seeds [42, 43, 44]
steps.step6: Save all pruned model checkpoints for MIA evaluation

title: Implement Taylor Importance Pruning Baseline
description: Task-only gradient saliency baseline from Molchanov et al. (CVPR 2019). Uses first-order Taylor expansion to estimate neuron importance for task performance.

steps.step1: Implement Taylor importance: S_task(i) = |E[(∂L/∂h_i) * h_i]| for each channel
steps.step2: Compute gradients on a mini-batch from training set (128 samples)
steps.step3: Rank channels by Taylor importance, prune lowest-ranked channels
steps.step4: Iterative pruning: 5 iterations, target sparsity / 5 per iteration
steps.step5: Fine-tune for 8 epochs after each pruning step (4 at lr=0.01, 4 at lr=0.001)
steps.step7: This baseline tests whether task-oriented gradient saliency helps privacy

title: Implement Hybrid Baseline: Magnitude + KL Regularization
description: CRITICAL BASELINE to isolate value of gradient-based neuron selection. Combines magnitude pruning with KL-divergence regularization without gradient-based neuron selection. This directly tests whether gradient-based selection adds value beyond magnitude+KL.

steps.step1: Use magnitude-based channel selection (same as magnitude pruning baseline)
steps.step2: During fine-tuning after each pruning step, add KL-divergence regularization loss
steps.step3: KL loss: L_KL = mean(KL(P_train || P_test)) where P is output softmax distribution
steps.step4: Implementation: Compute mean softmax outputs on train batch and validation batch, minimize KL divergence
steps.step5: Total loss: L = L_ce + λ * L_KL where λ=0.1
steps.step6: Same pruning schedule: 5 iterations, 8 epochs fine-tuning per iteration
steps.step8: Compare against G3P: If G3P wins, gradient-based selection adds value beyond magnitude+KL

title: Implement G3P Core: Privacy Saliency Metric Computation
description: Core contribution: Compute train-test gradient magnitude differences to identify privacy-leaking neurons. This is the novel privacy-specific formulation (distinct from general 'gradient-based saliency').

steps.step1: Implement gradient extraction: For each batch, compute ∂L/∂h_i for all channels h_i using torch.autograd.grad
steps.step4: - Sample 512 training examples in batches of 128, compute mean gradient magnitudes
steps.step5: - Sample 512 validation examples (held-out), compute mean gradient magnitudes
steps.step7: Task saliency: S_task(i) = |E_train[(∂L/∂h_i) * h_i]| (Taylor expansion from Molchanov)
steps.step8: Combined saliency: S_combined(i) = α * S_task(i) - β * S_privacy(i)
steps.step9: Default hyperparameters: α=1.0, β=1.0 (ablated in analysis experiments)

title: G3P Progressive Pruning and Fine-tuning
description: Execute G3P pruning at multiple sparsity levels. Progressive pruning allows the model to adapt gradually to the removal of privacy-leaking neurons.

steps.step3: - Compute privacy saliency using current model (train-test gradient difference)
steps.step4: - Compute task saliency using Taylor expansion
steps.step5: - Calculate combined saliency with α=1.0, β=1.0
steps.step6: - Sort channels by S_combined, prune lowest (target_sparsity / 5) percent per iteration
steps.step7: - Fine-tune for 8 epochs: 4 epochs lr=0.01, 4 epochs lr=0.001
steps.step8: - Add KL regularization during fine-tuning: L = L_ce + 0.1 * L_KL
steps.step9: - Repeat for 5 iterations to reach target_sparsity
steps.step10: Train with 3 seeds [42, 43, 44] for each sparsity level
steps.step11: Save checkpoints at each sparsity level and final model

title: Implement DP-SGD Baseline
description: Differential privacy baseline for comparison. DP-SGD provides strong privacy guarantees but with utility degradation. Uses Opacus library.

steps.step1: Configure DP-SGD using Opacus: max_grad_norm=1.0, noise_multiplier computed for target ε=4
steps.step2: Use privacy accountant (Rényi DP) to track ε during training
steps.step4: Train for 100 epochs with batch_size=256 (larger batches help DP-SGD, fewer epochs for time)
steps.step6: Record final (ε, δ) and compare utility-privacy trade-off against G3P

title: Implement Threshold-Based Membership Inference Attack
description: Fast MIA for all evaluations. Uses loss-based threshold to classify members vs non-members. Efficient (~3 min per model). This is the primary attack for comparing methods.

steps.step1: For each trained model, compute cross-entropy loss on 1,000 members (from train) and 1,000 non-members (from test)
steps.step2: Attack strategy: Lower loss → member, Higher loss → non-member
steps.step3: Find optimal threshold that maximizes attack accuracy using ROC curve
steps.step6: - AUC: Area under ROC curve (primary metric - lower is better)
steps.step7: - Attack Advantage: TPR at 0.1% FPR (following Carlini et al.)
steps.step8: Run on all baselines and G3P models at all sparsity levels

title: Implement and Run LiRA Membership Inference Attack
description: State-of-the-art MIA from Carlini et al. (IEEE S&P 2022). Uses likelihood ratio with shadow models. More accurate but computationally expensive. Reduced to 8 shadow models and run only on key comparisons to fit time budget (~60 min).

steps.step1: Train 8 shadow models (reduced from 16 for time) on random subsets of training data (80% splits)
steps.step2: For each target sample, compute likelihood under IN models (trained with sample) and OUT models (trained without)
steps.step3: LiRA score: log[P(loss | IN) / P(loss | OUT)] approximated using Gaussian over shadow model losses
steps.step4: For final validation only, run LiRA on these 4 key models (to save time):
steps.step9: Compute: AUC, TPR at 0.1% FPR, TPR at 1% FPR
steps.step10: Compare G3P vs baselines on LiRA (gold standard privacy metric)

title: Ablation Study: Alpha and Beta Hyperparameters
description: Test the impact of task-privacy trade-off parameters on the privacy-utility frontier. Reduced scope to fit time budget.

steps.step3: - α=1.0, β=0.0: Task-only (equivalent to Taylor pruning)
steps.step7: Measure: Test accuracy, MIA AUC (threshold attack)
steps.step8: Find optimal α/β trade-off for paper's main results

title: Ablation Study: Pruning vs Pruning + KL Regularization
description: Isolate the contribution of KL regularization fine-tuning after G3P pruning. Tests whether KL adds value beyond gradient-based selection.

steps.step1: G3P without KL: Only use combined saliency for pruning, standard fine-tuning (CE loss only)
steps.step2: G3P with KL: Combined saliency + KL regularization (default)
steps.step5: Determine if KL regularization adds value beyond gradient-based selection

title: Layer-wise Privacy Leakage Analysis
description: Analyze which layers contribute most to privacy leakage by measuring per-layer privacy saliency scores.

steps.step1: Compute S_privacy(i) for each channel in each layer of unpruned model
steps.step2: Aggregate by layer: mean privacy saliency per layer
steps.step3: Visualize: Bar plot of privacy leakage by layer
steps.step4: Identify: Which layers are most privacy-leaking?
steps.step5: Compare: Which layers does G3P prune most aggressively?

title: Correlation Analysis: Gradient Difference vs MIA Vulnerability
description: Validate that train-test gradient difference actually correlates with membership inference vulnerability at the neuron level. Samples 50 channels for computational efficiency.

steps.step1: For unpruned model, compute privacy saliency S_privacy for all channels
steps.step2: Randomly sample 50 channels across different layers
steps.step3: For each sampled channel, individually mask it (set to zero) and measure MIA AUC
steps.step4: Compute correlation: Pearson r between S_privacy and (1 - MIA_AUC after masking)
steps.step5: Also compute correlation between magnitude-based importance and MIA vulnerability
steps.step6: Hypothesis: S_privacy should correlate better with MIA vulnerability than magnitude
steps.step7: If correlation is weak, report honestly as limitation

title: Statistical Significance Testing
description: Perform rigorous statistical tests to confirm G3P improvements are significant.

steps.step1: Collect results from 3 random seeds for each method
steps.step2: Paired t-test: G3P vs Magnitude Pruning at same sparsity
steps.step3: Hypothesis: H0: μ_G3P_MIA_AUC >= μ_Magnitude_MIA_AUC, H1: μ_G3P_MIA_AUC < μ_Magnitude_MIA_AUC
steps.step5: Also test: G3P vs Hybrid (Magnitude + KL) - CRITICAL TEST
steps.step6: Report: t-statistic, p-value, effect size (Cohen's d)

title: Generate Privacy-Utility-Efficiency Frontier Plots
description: Create publication-quality figures showing the trade-offs between accuracy, privacy, and efficiency.

steps.step1: Plot 1: Privacy-Utility Frontier (Test Accuracy vs MIA AUC)
steps.step4: - Points: Unpruned, Magnitude (30/50/70%), Taylor (50%), Hybrid (30/50/70%), G3P (30/50/70%), DP-SGD
steps.step6: Plot 2: Sparsity vs MIA AUC - comparing G3P vs baselines
steps.step7: Plot 3: Sparsity vs Test Accuracy - showing utility preservation
steps.step8: Save as PDF for paper, PNG for quick viewing

title: Generate Ablation Study Visualizations
description: Visualize ablation results to show component contributions.

steps.step1: Bar plot: Alpha/Beta ablation results at 50% sparsity
steps.step2: - Grouped bars for different (α, β) configurations
steps.step3: - Show both test accuracy and MIA AUC
steps.step4: Bar plot: Component ablation (G3P vs G3P-no-KL vs Task-only vs Privacy-only)
steps.step6: Save all as PDF for paper

title: Final Results Summary and Success Criteria Check
description: Compile all results and check against success criteria defined in the proposal. Document whether hypothesis is confirmed or refuted.

steps.step1: Create results table with all methods and metrics:
steps.step2: - Method | Sparsity | Test Acc | MIA AUC (Threshold) | MIA AUC (LiRA) | FLOPs
steps.step3: Check Success Criteria 1: G3P significantly lower MIA AUC than magnitude (p < 0.05)?
steps.step4: Check Success Criteria 2: G3P outperforms hybrid baseline (magnitude + KL)? - CRITICAL
steps.step5: Check Success Criteria 3: G3P within 2% accuracy of unpruned at 30-50% sparsity?
steps.step6: Document any refutation conditions that occurred
steps.step7: Prepare summary for paper's results section
